questions.question: 1. What are the advantages of a doubly linked list?

answers.a: The memory required for a doubly linked list is less than a linked list
answers.b: Doubly linked list allows for traversing both sides.
answers.c: Inserting an element is faster in a doubly linked list.

explanations.a: Incorrect. Doubly linked lists require more memory than singly linked lists because each node stores two pointers (prev and next) instead of one.
explanations.b: Correct. Each node has pointers to both previous and next nodes, allowing traversal in both directions.
explanations.c: Incorrect. Insertion time is not inherently faster in doubly linked lists compared to singly linked lists.
explanations.d: Incorrect. The ability to traverse in both directions is a significant advantage.

questions.question: 2. What is the time complexity for insertion of an element into a doubly linked list (at a known position)?

explanations.a: Correct. Insertion at a known position (with a pointer to the node) in a doubly linked list takes constant time, O(1).
explanations.b: Incorrect. There is no logarithmic time operation for insertion in linked lists.
explanations.c: Incorrect. O(n) would be the time to find a position by value, but not for insertion at a known node.
explanations.d: Incorrect. O(n^2) is not applicable to insertion in linked lists.

questions.question: 3. Which of the following can make deletion in a doubly linked list a constant time operation?

answers.a: Keeping the doubly linked list sorted
answers.b: Having a pointer at both the first and last element
answers.c: Having the pointer of the node to be deleted
answers.d: Knowing the position of the node

explanations.a: Incorrect. Sorting does not affect the time complexity of deletion in a doubly linked list.
explanations.b: Incorrect. Having pointers to the ends only helps with quick access to those nodes, not arbitrary deletions.
explanations.c: Correct. If you have a pointer to the node to be deleted, you can remove it in constant time by updating the previous and next pointers.
explanations.d: Incorrect. Knowing the position is not enough; you still need to traverse to that node, which takes O(n) time.
